text: Hi everyone. Small bowel obstruction is one of the most common causes of general surgical emergency presentations. In this video, we will show the laparoscopic treatment for a small bowel obstruction. The video will also demonstrate current guidelines dealing with laparoscopic treatment for small bowel obstruction. If you like this video, please subscribe to my channel by pressing the red button. Thank you. To our knowledge, the two up-to-date current guidelines dealing with this condition are the Bologna Guidelines and the American Association of Surgery of Trauma Guidelines for small bowel obstruction treatment. Please refer to these useful resources.

This evidence-based algorithm from the Bologna guidelines will take you in a stepwise fashion from conservative management to the surgical approach required necessary for small bowel obstruction. It also delineates the investigations required for these conditions. In summary, an initial conservative approach is recommended unless the patient is showing signs of peritonitis, strangulation or a bowel ischemia. A CT scan with a water-soluble contrast enema is a diagnostic modality of choice. The conservative management with the quote-unquote drip-and-suck approach should last between forty-eight to seventy-two hours. and amongst this the patient should be carefully monitored for any signs of ischemia or bowel strangulation. If the conservative management or non-operative treatment has failed the patient should be considered for surgery. The traditional laparotomy and adhesiolosis has recently been challenged by diagnostic laparoscopy and division of adhesions. Patients for laparoscopic adhesiolosis should be chosen carefully as this approach has got a high morbidity and sometimes mortality.

The surgeon uses a supraumbilical cut down and inserts a blunt trocar into the abdominal cavity, taking care to avoid injury to the dilated and fragile bowel. The ports are inserted under direct vision, with the open jaws of the hands ensuring safe entry. The division of adhesions is performed with scissors, keeping the tip pointed towards the abdominal wall to prevent enterotomy. The surgeon identifies the transition point between the collapsed and dilated loops of the small bowel, noting the pale mucosa of the collapsed bowel and the hyperemic red mucosa of the dilated bowel. A band has been divided, clearly demonstrating the transition point.

The surgeon assesses the bowel viability on both sides of the constriction ring, indicating a positive outcome. The CT scan revealed a closed loop obstruction, prompting the search for a second transition point. Upon examination, the proximal bowel shows a thickened and scarred mesentery near the SMA and SMV pedicle, with the bowel twisted around it. The twist is unraveled, exposing the scarred area, but further dissection is avoided to prevent compromising blood supply. Additional adhesions are carefully removed to prevent future complications.

Be prepared to do intracorporeal suturing if you have detected any serosal tears or enterotomies. We will not recommend the use of prosthetic material if required in such surgeries.
